On "the LORD spake unto Moses," the reformed and witnesses note the revelation. Gill notes the LORD speaking (the revelation of the tabernacle, the pattern, vv.9) — the divine instruction; the word that began the sanctuary.

On the word, Keil and the grammatical-historical note the design: the LORD revealing the tabernacle (the design, the pattern, the furniture) — the place of God's dwelling, the meeting of God and his people, vv.8, 22; the providence (the revelation of the sanctuary, the type of heavenly things, Hebrews 8:5; 9:23-24). The verse displays the LORD beginning the revelation of the tabernacle.

2:
"Speak unto the children of Israel, that they bring me an offering: of every man that giveth it willingly with his heart ye shall take my offering." The witnesses note the freewill offering for the sanctuary — the crux of the willing heart. On "Speak unto the children of Israel, that they bring me an offering," the grammatical voices note the offering. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "speak unto the children of Israel, that they bring me an offering: of every man that giveth it willingly with his heart ye shall take my offering" — God called the people to bring an offering (a contribution, the materials for the sanctuary); the offering (the freewill gift for the building of the tabernacle, vv.3-7); the call (the people invited to give for God's house).

On "of every man that giveth it willingly with his heart," interpreters expound the willingness. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the willing heart — "of every man that giveth it willingly with his heart ye shall take my offering": the offering was to be freewill, from a willing heart (not compelled, but cheerfully and voluntarily given); the witnesses note the principle — God loves the willing, cheerful giver (the offering acceptable when given from the heart, 2 Corinthians 9:7, "God loveth a cheerful giver"; not grudgingly, or of necessity); the lesson — the spirit of acceptable giving (the heart, not the amount; the willing mind, 2 Corinthians 8:12, "if there be first a willing mind, it is accepted"); the freewill offering for God's house (the people's hearts stirred to give, Exodus 35:5, 21-22, 29, "as many as were willing hearted"; 1 Chronicles 29:9, 14, "of thine own have we given thee"); the gladness of giving to God.

On "ye shall take my offering," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the acceptance. Wesley and Ellicott note "my offering" (the offering taken of the willing) — God's offering (given to him, accepted by him); the providence (the willing offering acceptable, the heart the measure). The verse displays the freewill offering for the sanctuary.

The witnesses agree the substance: the offering was to be freewill, of every willing heart (Calvin, Wesley, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the willing heart: "of every man that giveth it willingly with his heart" — the freewill offering for God's house, given not by compulsion but from a willing, cheerful heart (2 Corinthians 9:7; 8:12), the spirit of acceptable giving (the heart, not the amount), the gladness of giving to God (Exodus 35:5, 21-22; 1 Chronicles 29:9, 14). The verse displays the materials of the offering (vv.3-7).

8:
"And let them make me a sanctuary; that I may dwell among them." The witnesses note the sanctuary that God may dwell among his people — the crux of the indwelling God. On "let them make me a sanctuary," the grammatical voices note the sanctuary. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "let them make me a sanctuary; that I may dwell among them" — God commanded Israel to make him a sanctuary (a holy place, the tabernacle); the sanctuary (the holy dwelling, the place of God's presence among his people); the great purpose (vv.8b, that God may dwell among them).

On "that I may dwell among them," interpreters expound the great purpose. The reformed and the dominant reading note the wonder — "that I may dwell among them": the purpose of the sanctuary, that God himself might dwell in the midst of his people; the witnesses note the astonishing condescension — the high and holy God, who dwells in the heaven of heavens (1 Kings 8:27), and who appeared on the mount as devouring fire (Exodus 24:17), stooping to dwell among sinful men; the indwelling God (God in the midst of his people, the great blessing of the covenant, the presence of God with them, Exodus 29:45-46, "I will dwell among the children of Israel, and will be their God"; Leviticus 26:11-12); the lesson — the dwelling of God with man, the central wonder, advancing through Scripture: the tabernacle, the temple, then the incarnation (the Word made flesh, and "dwelt (tabernacled) among us," John 1:14), then the church (the temple of God, the Spirit dwelling in them, 1 Corinthians 3:16; Ephesians 2:21-22), and at last the consummation ("the tabernacle of God is with men, and he will dwell with them," Revelation 21:3); the heart of the gospel, Immanuel, God with us (Matthew 1:23).

On the sanctuary, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the presence: the sanctuary made that God may dwell among them — the presence of God the glory of the sanctuary; the providence (the dwelling of God with his people, the type of the greater dwelling in Christ and the church and the glory). The verse displays the sanctuary that God may dwell among his people.

Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "according to all that I shew thee, after the pattern of the tabernacle, and the pattern of all the instruments thereof, even so shall ye make it" — the tabernacle and all its instruments (furniture) were to be made exactly according to the pattern God showed Moses on the mount; the pattern (the divine model, the heavenly pattern, vv.40); the exactness (nothing left to human invention, all according to God's design); the divine authorship of the sanctuary.

On "after the pattern of the tabernacle, and the pattern of all the instruments thereof," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the model. Calvin and Gill note the pattern (the model shown to Moses, the heavenly original, Hebrews 8:5, "the pattern shewed to thee in the mount") — the divine design; the tabernacle a copy of heavenly things (the witnesses note, Hebrews 9:23-24, "the patterns of things in the heavens").

On "even so shall ye make it," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the exactness: "even so shall ye make it" — the exact obedience (the sanctuary made precisely as shown, nothing added or omitted); the lesson (the worship of God to be according to his appointment, not man's invention, the regulative principle; the will-worship condemned, Colossians 2:23); the providence (the pattern divine, the tabernacle a figure of heavenly things, the type of Christ and the church, Hebrews 8:5; 9:23-24). 40:
"And look that thou make them after their pattern, which was shewed thee in the mount." The witnesses note the charge to make all according to the pattern in the mount — the crux of the heavenly pattern. On "look that thou make them after their pattern," the grammatical voices note the charge. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "look that thou make them after their pattern, which was shewed thee in the mount" — Moses was charged to make all the furniture (and the tabernacle) exactly after the pattern shown him in the mount; the charge (the exact conformity to the divine model); the pattern (the heavenly original, shown to Moses, vv.9); the warning ("look," take heed, that nothing be added or altered).

On "after their pattern," interpreters expound the pattern. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the heavenly pattern — the model shown to Moses in the mount; the witnesses note the New Testament use — the Epistle to the Hebrews cites this very word to prove the tabernacle a copy of heavenly things: "who serve unto the example and shadow of heavenly things, as Moses was admonished of God when he was about to make the tabernacle: for, See, saith he, that thou make all things according to the pattern shewed to thee in the mount" (Hebrews 8:5); the tabernacle a figure, a copy, a shadow of the true, the heavenly sanctuary (Hebrews 9:23-24, "the patterns of things in the heavens... the figures of the true; but into heaven itself"); the lesson — the earthly sanctuary modeled on the heavenly, a shadow of Christ and the heavenly realities, the worship of God according to his appointment (not man's invention); the regulative principle (all according to the pattern, nothing of man's devising).

On "which was shewed thee in the mount," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the model: "which was shewed thee in the mount" — the pattern shown in the mount (the heavenly model, vv.9); the providence (the tabernacle a copy of heavenly things, the shadow of Christ and the true sanctuary, Hebrews 8:5; 9:23-24; the worship according to God's appointment). The verse displays the charge to make all according to the pattern in the mount.
